title: Present perfect: write the correct form
lesson: The present perfect (have/has + past participle) connects the past to now: life experience, recent news, and situations continuing up to the present. Pair it with 'just', 'already', 'never', 'for', and 'since' — never with a finished past time like 'yesterday'.

items.explanation: Present perfect = have/has + past participle; 'just' goes between: have just finished.
For a situation continuing up to now with 'for', use the present perfect: has lived.
Life experience uses the present perfect; 'never' comes before the participle: have never visited.
'lose' is irregular (lost), and 'already' sits between 'has' and the participle.
'today' is unfinished time, so use the present perfect; write → written.
'since' marks the start of a period continuing to now, so use the present perfect; know → known.
In questions about life experience with 'ever', the participle follows 'have you': eat → eaten.
'yet' goes at the end of negatives and questions to mean 'up to now': hasn't arrived yet.

items.text: My parents {1} to Paris; they're there right now.
items.explanation: 'have gone' means they went and are still away. (Use 'been' only for a completed visit they've returned from.)

items.text: She {1} to Egypt twice, but she's home now.
items.explanation: 'has been' describes a completed experience (she went and came back); 'gone' would mean she is still there.
'last week' is finished past time, so use the past simple, not the present perfect; see → saw.

items.text: He {1} his report, so he can relax now.
items.explanation: 'already' (before the participle) stresses that something happened sooner than expected; finish → finished.
For a completed visit you have returned from, use 'been' (not 'gone') in present perfect experience questions with 'ever': Have you ever been...?

items.text: He {1} for the same company for twenty years.
items.explanation: 'for' + a length of time (for twenty years) marks a situation continuing up to now, so use the present perfect: has worked.

items.text: They {1} their old car three years ago.
items.explanation: 'ago' points to a finished moment in the past, so use the past simple, not the present perfect; sell → sold.
